revision.text:
'''白鳥陵'''（しらとりのみささぎ）は、[[ヤマトタケル]]の陵である。
[[能褒野王塚古墳]]（[[三重県]][[亀山市]]）と[[軽里大塚古墳]]（[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]）の二つが比定されている。
==概要== ヤマトタケルが伊勢国能褒野で薨じ、同地に「陵」を造って葬ったことは、「古事記」「日本書紀」ともに一致するところであるが、さらに白鳥（古事記では八尋白智鳥）と化して西方に飛び、降り留まった地にあらたに「陵」を起こしたという記事は両書で異同がある。 古事記においては能褒野から白鳥となって飛び、河内国志幾に留まり、そこに「陵」を起こし、これを白鳥陵と呼んだが、のちここからまたも白鳥となって飛び、ついに昇天したという。 つまり古事記の白鳥陵は河内国志幾に営んだ「陵」である。 日本書紀巻第七によれば白鳥となって能褒野陵から出て、まず大和国琴弾原にとどまり、そこに「陵」を造ったところ、さらに白鳥となって河内国旧市邑にいきとどまったので、そこにも「陵」を造ったが、また白鳥となって天に上ったという。 日本書紀では最初の能褒野陵、大和琴弾原の「陵」、河内国旧市邑の「陵」と三陵を時人は「白鳥陵」とよんだという。 皇子の墓を「陵」というのは、古事記、日本書紀においてヤマトタケルの能褒野陵、2つの白鳥陵のみで、例外的である。 のちにヤマトタケルの墓を能褒野墓と定め、また日本書紀の所伝をも尊重し、「白鳥陵」2つを能褒野墓に附属するものとした。
{{substub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しらとりのみささき}} [[Category:日本の陵墓]]

'''白鳥陵'''（しらとりのみささぎ）は、[[ヤマトタケル]]の陵である。
[[能褒野王塚古墳]]（[[三重県]][[亀山市]]）と[[軽里大塚古墳]]（[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]）の二つが比定されている。
==概要== ヤマトタケルが伊勢国能褒野で薨じ、同地に「陵」を造って葬ったことは、「古事記」「日本書紀」ともに一致するところであるが、さらに白鳥（古事記では八尋白智鳥）と化して西方に飛び、降り留まった地にあらたに「陵」を起こしたという記事は両書で異同がある。 古事記においては能褒野から白鳥となって飛び、河内国志幾に留まり、そこに「陵」を起こし、これを白鳥陵と呼んだが、のちここからまたも白鳥となって飛び、ついに昇天したという。 つまり古事記の白鳥陵は河内国志幾に営んだ「陵」である。 日本書紀巻第七によれば白鳥となって能褒野陵から出て、まず大和国琴弾原にとどまり、そこに「陵」を造ったところ、さらに白鳥となって河内国旧市邑にいきとどまったので、そこにも「陵」を造ったが、また白鳥となって天に上ったという。 日本書紀では最初の能褒野陵、大和琴弾原の「陵」、河内国旧市邑の「陵」と三陵を時人は「白鳥陵」とよんだという。 皇子の墓を「陵」というのは、古事記、日本書紀においてヤマトタケルの能褒野陵、2つの白鳥陵のみで、例外的である。 のちにヤマトタケルの墓を能褒野墓と定め、また日本書紀の所伝をも尊重し、「白鳥陵」2つを能褒野墓に附属するものとした。
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しらとりのみささき}} [[Category:日本の陵墓]]

{{出典の明記|date=2011年3月}} '''白鳥陵'''（しらとりのみささぎ）は、[[ヤマトタケル]]の陵である。
[[能褒野王塚古墳]]（[[三重県]][[亀山市]]）と[[軽里大塚古墳]]（[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]）の二つが比定されている。
==概要== ヤマトタケルが伊勢国能褒野で薨じ、同地に「陵」を造って葬ったことは、「古事記」「日本書紀」ともに一致するところであるが、さらに白鳥（古事記では八尋白智鳥）と化して西方に飛び、降り留まった地にあらたに「陵」を起こしたという記事は両書で異同がある。 古事記においては能褒野から白鳥となって飛び、河内国志幾に留まり、そこに「陵」を起こし、これを白鳥陵と呼んだが、のちここからまたも白鳥となって飛び、ついに昇天したという。 つまり古事記の白鳥陵は河内国志幾に営んだ「陵」である。 日本書紀巻第七によれば白鳥となって能褒野陵から出て、まず大和国琴弾原（[[奈良県]][[御所市]]冨田）にとどまり、そこに「陵」を造ったところ、さらに白鳥となって河内国旧市邑にいきとどまったので、そこにも「陵」を造ったが、また白鳥となって天に上ったという。 日本書紀では最初の能褒野陵、大和琴弾原の「陵」、河内国旧市邑の「陵」と三陵を時人は「白鳥陵」とよんだという。 皇子の墓を「陵」というのは、古事記、日本書紀においてヤマトタケルの能褒野陵、2つの白鳥陵のみで、例外的である。 のちにヤマトタケルの墓を能褒野墓と定め、また日本書紀の所伝をも尊重し、「白鳥陵」2つを能褒野墓に附属するものとした。
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html 羽曳野市 白鳥陵古墳] *[http://www.city.gose.nara.jp/gyousei/kikaku/4yamatake01.html 御所市 白鳥伝説と三市交流の合意]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しらとりのみささき}} [[Category:日本の陵墓]]

{{出典の明記|date=2011年3月}} '''白鳥陵'''（しらとりのみささぎ）は、[[ヤマトタケル]]の陵である。
[[能褒野王塚古墳]]（[[三重県]][[亀山市]]）と[[軽里大塚古墳]]（[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]）の二つが比定されている。
==概要== ヤマトタケルが[[伊勢国]]能褒野で薨じ、同地に「陵」を造って葬ったことは、『古事記』『日本書紀』ともに一致するところであるが、さらに白鳥（『古事記』では「八尋白智鳥」）と化して西方に飛び、降り留まった地にあらたに「陵」を起こしたという記事は両書で異同がある。
『古事記』においては能褒野から白鳥となって飛び、[[河内国]]志幾に留まり、そこに「陵」を起こし、これを「白鳥陵」と呼んだが、のちここからまたも白鳥となって飛び、ついに昇天したという。 つまり『古事記』の白鳥陵は河内国志幾に営んだ「陵」である。
『日本書紀』巻第七によれば白鳥となって能褒野陵から出て、まず[[大和国]]琴弾原（[[奈良県]][[御所市]]冨田）にとどまり、そこに「陵」を造ったところ、さらに白鳥となって河内国旧市邑にいきとどまったので、そこにも「陵」を造ったが、また白鳥となって天に上ったという。 『日本書紀』では最初の能褒野陵、大和琴弾原の「陵」、河内国旧市邑の「陵」と三陵を時人は「白鳥陵」とよんだという。
皇子の墓を「陵」というのは、『古事記』、『日本書紀』においてヤマトタケルの能褒野陵、2つの白鳥陵のみで、例外的である。
のちにヤマトタケルの墓を「能褒野墓」と定め、また『日本書紀』の所伝をも尊重し、「白鳥陵」2つを能褒野墓に附属するものとした。
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html 羽曳野市 白鳥陵古墳] *[http://www.city.gose.nara.jp/gyousei/kikaku/4yamatake01.html 御所市 白鳥伝説と三市交流の合意]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しらとりのみささき}} [[Category:日本の陵墓]]

{{出典の明記|date=2011年3月}} '''白鳥陵'''（しらとりのみささぎ）は、[[ヤマトタケル]]の陵。
[[奈良県]][[御所市]]富田と[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]軽里（[[軽里大塚古墳]]）の2ヶ所に治定されている。

『日本書紀』巻第七によれば白鳥となって能褒野陵から出て、まず[[大和国]]琴弾原（[[奈良県]][[御所市]]冨田）にとどまり、そこに「陵」を造ったところ、さらに白鳥となって河内国旧市邑にいきとどまったので、そこにも「陵」を造ったが、また白鳥となって天に上ったという。 『日本書紀』では最初の能褒野陵、大和琴弾原の「陵」、河内国旧市邑の「陵」と三陵を時人は「白鳥陵」とよんだという。

『古事記』においては能褒野から白鳥となって飛び、[[河内国]]志幾に留まり、そこに「陵」を起こし、これを「白鳥陵」と呼んだが、のちここからまたも白鳥となって飛び、ついに昇天したという。 つまり『古事記』の白鳥陵は河内国志幾に営んだ「陵」である。
『日本書紀』巻第七によれば白鳥となって能褒野陵から出て、まず[[大和国]]琴弾原（[[奈良県]][[御所市]]冨田）にとどまり、そこに「陵」を造ったところ、さらに白鳥となって河内国旧市邑にいきとどまったので、そこにも「陵」を造ったが、また白鳥となって天に上ったという。 『日本書紀』では最初の能褒野陵、大和琴弾原の「陵」、河内国旧市邑の「陵」と三陵を時人は「白鳥陵」とよんだという。
皇子の墓を「陵」というのは、『古事記』、『日本書紀』においてヤマトタケルの能褒野陵、2つの白鳥陵のみで、例外的である。
のちにヤマトタケルの墓を「能褒野墓」と定め、また『日本書紀』の所伝をも尊重し、「白鳥陵」2つを能褒野墓に附属するものとした。
== 備考 == * 『[[続日本紀]]』[[大宝 (日本)|大宝]]2年（[[702年]]）8月8日条に、「倭建命の墓に落雷があったので、使いを遣わして鎮祭をした」と記述がみられる。
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html 羽曳野市 白鳥陵古墳] *[http://www.city.gose.nara.jp/gyousei/kikaku/4yamatake01.html 御所市 白鳥伝説と三市交流の合意]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しらとりのみささき}} [[Category:日本の陵墓]] [[Category:ヤマトタケル伝説|墓しらとりのみささき]]

皇子の墓を「陵」というのは、『古事記』、『日本書紀』においてヤマトタケルの能褒野陵、2つの白鳥陵のみで、例外的である。

It is designated in two locations: Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳}}), {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Osaka Prefecture. == Overview == Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where a "tomb..."
revision.text:
{{Cite source|date=March 2011}} '''Shiratori Ryo''' (白鳥陵) is a tomb associated with {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}.
It is designated in two locations: Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳}}), {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the {{ill|Taihō|ja|大宝}} era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."
== External links == * [http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html Shiratori Ryo Tumulus, Habikino City] * [https://www.city.gose.nara.jp/0000000428.html Yamato Takeru and the Shiratori Legend - Friendship Exchange Program Between Three Cities]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
[[Category:Imperial tombs of Japan]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[ja:白鳥陵]]

revision.text:
{{Cite source|date=March 2011}} '''Shiratori Ryo''' (白鳥陵) is a tomb associated with {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}.
It is designated in two locations: Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳}}), {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the {{ill|Taihō|ja|大宝}} era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."

revision.text:
{{Cite source|date=March 2011}} '''Shiratori Ryo''' (白鳥陵) is a tomb associated with {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}.
It is designated in two locations: Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳}}), {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the {{ill|Taihō|ja|大宝}} era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."
== External links == * [http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html Shiratori Ryo Tumulus, Habikino City] * [https://www.city.gose.nara.jp/0000000428.html Yamato Takeru and the Shiratori Legend - Friendship Exchange Program Between Three Cities]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{Cite source|date=March 2011}} '''Shiratori Ryo''' (白鳥陵) is a tomb associated with {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}.
It is designated in two locations: Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳}}), {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.

It is designated in two locations: Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳}}), {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.

{{Cite source|date=March 20qq}} '''Shiratori Ryo''' (白鳥陵) is a tomb associated with {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}.
It is designated in two locations: Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳}}), {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the {{ill|Taihō|ja|大宝}} era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."
== External links == * [http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html Shiratori Ryo Tumulus, Habikino City] * [https://www.city.gose.nara.jp/0000000428.html Yamato Takeru and the Shiratori Legend - Friendship Exchange Program Between Three Cities]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the {{ill|Taihō|ja|大宝}} era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."
== External links == * [http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html Shiratori Ryo Kofun, Habikino City] * [https://www.city.gose.nara.jp/0000000428.html Yamato Takeru and the Shiratori Legend - Friendship Exchange Program Between Three Cities]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

It is designated in two locations: Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳}}), {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.

He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the {{ill|Taihō|ja|大宝}} era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."

{{Cite source|date=March 2011}} '''Shiratori Ryo''' (白鳥陵) is a tomb associated with {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル|12=draft}}.
It is designated in two locations: Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft}}, Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳|12=draft}}), {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft}}. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the {{ill|Taihō|ja|大宝|12=draft}} era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."

revision.text:
{{Cite source|date=March 2011}} '''Shiratori Ryo''' (白鳥陵) is a tomb associated with {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル|12=draft|12=draft}}.
It is designated in two locations: Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}), {{ill|Habikino, Osaka|ja|羽曳野市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft|12=draft}}. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the {{ill|Taihō|ja|大宝|12=draft|12=draft}} era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."
== External links == * [http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html Shiratori Ryo Kofun, Habikino City] * [https://www.city.gose.nara.jp/0000000428.html Yamato Takeru and the Shiratori Legend - Friendship Exchange Program Between Three Cities]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{draft categories|
}}

[[sn:Shiratori Ryo]] [[ja:白鳥陵]] [[sn:hiratori Ryo]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Shiratori Ryo}}

revision.comment: 14 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:白鳥陵 up to rev 86092564
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2011年3月}} '''白鳥陵'''（しらとりのみささぎ）は、[[ヤマトタケル]]の陵。
[[奈良県]][[御所市]]富田と[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]軽里（[[軽里大塚古墳]]）の2ヶ所に治定されている。
==概要== ヤマトタケルが[[伊勢国]]能褒野で薨じ、同地に「陵」を造って葬ったことは、『古事記』『日本書紀』ともに一致するところであるが、さらに白鳥（『古事記』では「八尋白智鳥」）と化して西方に飛び、降り留まった地にあらたに「陵」を起こしたという記事は両書で異同がある。
『古事記』においては能褒野から白鳥となって飛び、[[河内国]]志幾に留まり、そこに「陵」を起こし、これを「白鳥陵」と呼んだが、のちここからまたも白鳥となって飛び、ついに昇天したという。 つまり『古事記』の白鳥陵は河内国志幾に営んだ「陵」である。
『日本書紀』巻第七によれば白鳥となって能褒野陵から出て、まず[[大和国]]琴弾原（[[奈良県]][[御所市]]冨田）にとどまり、そこに「陵」を造ったところ、さらに白鳥となって河内国旧市邑にいきとどまったので、そこにも「陵」を造ったが、また白鳥となって天に上ったという。 『日本書紀』では最初の能褒野陵、大和琴弾原の「陵」、河内国旧市邑の「陵」と三陵を時人は「白鳥陵」とよんだという。
皇子の墓を「陵」というのは、『古事記』、『日本書紀』においてヤマトタケルの能褒野陵、2つの白鳥陵のみで、例外的である。
のちにヤマトタケルの墓を「能褒野墓」と定め、また『日本書紀』の所伝をも尊重し、「白鳥陵」2つを能褒野墓に附属するものとした。
== 備考 == * 『[[続日本紀]]』[[大宝 (日本)|大宝]]2年（[[702年]]）8月8日条に、「倭建命の墓に落雷があったので、使いを遣わして鎮祭をした」と記述がみられる。
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html 羽曳野市 白鳥陵古墳] *[https://www.city.gose.nara.jp/0000000428.html 日本武尊・白鳥伝説三市交流事業 白鳥がとりもつ三市友好交流]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しらとりのみささき}} [[Category:日本の陵墓]] [[Category:ヤマトタケル]]

==概要== ヤマトタケルが[[伊勢国]]能褒野で薨じ、同地に「陵」を造って葬ったことは、『古事記』『日本書紀』ともに一致するところであるが、さらに白鳥（『古事記』では「八尋白智鳥」）と化して西方に飛び、降り留まった地にあらたに「陵」を起こしたという記事は両書で異同がある。
『古事記』においては能褒野から白鳥となって飛び、[[河内国]]志幾に留まり、そこに「陵」を起こし、これを「白鳥陵」と呼んだが、のちここからまたも白鳥となって飛び、ついに昇天したという。 つまり『古事記』の白鳥陵は河内国志幾に営んだ「陵」である。

{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しらとりのみささき}} [[Category:日本の陵墓]] [[Category:ヤマトタケル]]

Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (present-day Tomita, {{ill|Gose, Nara|ja|御所市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the {{ill|Taihō|ja|大宝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."
== External links == * [http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html Shiratori Ryo Kofun, Habikino City] * [https://www.city.gose.nara.jp/0000000428.html Yamato Takeru and the Shiratori Legend - Friendship Exchange Program Between Three Cities]

{{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Shiratori Ryo''' (白鳥陵) is a tomb associated with [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]].
It is designated in two locations: Tomita, [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Kariguri Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Kariguri Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), [[:en:Habikino|Habikino, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (present-day Tomita, [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō]] era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."
== External links == * [http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html Shiratori Ryo Kofun, Habikino City] * [https://www.city.gose.nara.jp/0000000428.html Yamato Takeru and the Shiratori Legend - Friendship Exchange Program Between Three Cities]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|白鳥陵|version=86092564|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Imperial tombs of Japan]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]]
}}

He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Both the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (present-day Tomita, [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō]] era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:白鳥陵]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11580607]] Wikidata name: Shiratori no Misasagi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Shiratori Ryo''' (白鳥陵) is a tomb associated with [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]].
It is designated in two locations: Tomita, [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Kariguri Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Kariguri Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), [[:en:Habikino|Habikino, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (present-day Tomita, [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō]] era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."
== External links == * [http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html Shiratori Ryo Kofun, Habikino City] * [https://www.city.gose.nara.jp/0000000428.html Yamato Takeru and the Shiratori Legend - Friendship Exchange Program Between Three Cities]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|白鳥陵|version=86092564|comment=Imported full ja history}}

}}

It is designated in two locations: Tomita, [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Kariguri Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Kariguri Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), [[:en:Habikino|Habikino, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (present-day Tomita, [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō]] era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."
== External links == * [http://www.kiis.or.jp/kansaida/habikino/habikino13.html Shiratori Ryo Kofun, Habikino City] * [https://www.city.gose.nara.jp/0000000428.html Yamato Takeru and the Shiratori Legend - Friendship Exchange Program Between Three Cities]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō]] era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."

It is designated in two locations: Tomita, [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nara Prefecture, and Karisato ({{ill|Kariguri Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Kariguri Ōtsuka Kofun|lt=Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|軽里大塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karisato Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), [[:en:Habikino|Habikino, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== Overview ==
Yamato Takeru is said to have passed away in Nobono, [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]], where a "tomb" was constructed to bury him. Both the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] agree on this point. However, both texts describe how he transformed into a white bird (referred to as "Yahiro Shirotomochi no Tori" in the *Kojiki*) and flew westward, where additional "tombs" were constructed at the locations where he alighted. The details differ between the two accounts.
In the *Kojiki*, after transforming into a white bird, Yamato Takeru flew from Nobono to Shiki in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]. A tomb was constructed there and called the "Shiratori Ryo." Later, he transformed into a white bird again and ascended to the heavens. Thus, according to the *Kojiki*, the Shiratori Ryo refers to the tomb constructed in Shiki, Kawachi Province.
According to *Nihon Shoki* Volume 7, Yamato Takeru became a white bird and departed from Nobono no Misasagi. He first alighted at Kotohikihara in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] (present-day Tomita, [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], Nara Prefecture), where a tomb was constructed. He then flew again to Kyūichimura in Kawachi Province, where another tomb was built. Finally, he ascended to the heavens. *Nihon Shoki* states that people of the time referred to the three tombs—the original Nobono no Misasagi, the tomb at Kotohikihara, and the one at Kyūichimura—as "Shiratori Ryo."
Referring to the tombs of imperial princes as "Ryo" (tombs) is rare in the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki*, with Yamato Takeru's Nobono no Misasagi and the two Shiratori Ryo being exceptions.
Later, Yamato Takeru's tomb was officially designated as the "Nobono no Haka," and the two Shiratori Ryo were considered affiliated with it, respecting the tradition described in *Nihon Shoki*.
== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the [[:en:Taiho|Taihō]] era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."

== Notes == * The *Shoku Nihongi* mentions in an entry dated August 8, 702 (second year of the {{ill|Taiho|en|Taiho|WD=Q423370|lt=Taihō}} era) that "lightning struck the tomb of Yamato Takeru, and emissaries were dispatched to conduct a purification ceremony."
